coredata.dc:title: Cascade selective recognition of H2O2 and ascorbic acid in living cells using carbon-based nanozymes with peroxidase-like activity
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Chaoqun
Cheng, Rumei
Liu, Bin
Fang, Yuying
Nan, Kaihui
Wu, Wencan
Xu, Yixuan
coredata.dc:description: The design of optical nanosensors based on carbon nanozymes has gained considerable interest in analyte detection. In this study, a simple and rapid fluorescence/colorimetry probe, NH2GQDs/o-phenylenediamine (OPD), was constructed by combining amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (NH2GQDs) and o-phenylenediamine. In the presence of NH2GQDs/H2O2, OPD was oxidized to 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP), giving fluorescent and colorimetric signals, which can be used to quantitatively measure H2O2. Furthermore, NH2GQDs/OPD/ H2O2 exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity for detecting ascorbic acid (AA) with a detection limit of 0.062 μM using the fluorescent method. Its selectivity for AA was ascribed to the formation of the condensation compound of DAP with dehydroascorbic acid rather than the quinoxaline path for dopamine and glucose. The colorimetric sensing system NH2GQDs/OPD displayed yellow in the presence of H2O2, while it changed to green upon adding AA. This sensing system was successfully applied for imaging H2O2 and AA in human cervical cancer cells and onion tissues. Additionally, the flexible sensing system can be used to fabricate the logic gate “AND.” The results provide a valuable method for its potential application in the biosensing, clinical medicine, and food industries.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Transmission electron microscopic image, (b) FTIR spectrum, (c) XRD characterization, and (d) Raman spectrum of the NH2GQDs.

(a) Electron paramagnetic resonance spectra of different systems with (black line) and without (red line) the NH2GQDs in the presence of 100 mM H2O2 and using 100 mM DMPO as the spin trap. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of various reaction systems: NH2GQDs, TMB, NH2GQDs/H2O2, TMB/H2O2, TMB/NH2GQDs, and TMB/NH2GQDs/H2O2; (c) corresponding color changes.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the NH2GQD/OPD sensing system in the presence of various H2O2 concentrations; (b) calibration curve for H2O2 detection; (c) fluorescence spectra of the NH2GQD/OPD/H2O2 sensing system in the presence of various AA concentrations; (d) corresponding calibration curve of AA detection; (e) UV–vis absorption spectra of the NH2GQD/OPD solution upon addition of various H2O2 concentrations; (f) linear relation between UV–vis absorption intensity and H2O2 concentration; (g) UV–vis absorption spectra of the NH2GQD/OPD/H2O2 solution upon addition of various AA concentrations; (h) linear relation between UV–vis absorption intensity with AA concentration.

Fluorescence microscopic images of living HeLa cells cultured with (i) NH2GQDs and (ii) and (iii) NH2GQDs/OPD for 2 h, followed by the addition of different concentrations of H2O2 for 0.5 h. Fluorescence images were acquired for blue and red channels under 400 nm excitation. The blue and red channels lie in the ranges of 450–510 nm and 550–610 nm, respectively. The scale bar is 50 µm.

Confocal fluorescence images of living HeLa cells after incubation with the NH2GQDs, followed by the addition of OPD/H2O2 for 0.5 h, then AA with various concentrations: (a) 0 μM, (b) 5 μM, and (c) 10 μM. The images were recorded in different channels under-400 nm excitation. Green channel: 510–550 nm; red channel: 550–610 nm (Scale bar: 50 µm).
(a) Preparation process of the NH2GQDs. (b) Cascade detection of H2O2 and AA using the NH2GQDs/OPD as a fluorescence/colorimetry probe.

head.ce:title: Cascade selective recognition of H2O2 and ascorbic acid in living cells using carbon-based nanozymes with peroxidase-like activity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design of optical nanosensors based on carbon nanozymes has gained considerable interest in analyte detection. In this study, a simple and rapid fluorescence/colorimetry probe, NH2GQDs/o-phenylenediamine (OPD), was constructed by combining amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (NH2GQDs) and o-phenylenediamine. In the presence of NH2GQDs/H2O2, OPD was oxidized to 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP), giving fluorescent and colorimetric signals, which can be used to quantitatively measure H2O2. Furthermore, NH2GQDs/OPD/ H2O2 exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity for detecting ascorbic acid (AA) with a detection limit of 0.062 μM using the fluorescent method. Its selectivity for AA was ascribed to the formation of the condensation compound of DAP with dehydroascorbic acid rather than the quinoxaline path for dopamine and glucose. The colorimetric sensing system NH2GQDs/OPD displayed yellow in the presence of H2O2, while it changed to green upon adding AA. This sensing system was successfully applied for imaging H2O2 and AA in human cervical cancer cells and onion tissues. Additionally, the flexible sensing system can be used to fabricate the logic gate “AND.” The results provide a valuable method for its potential application in the biosensing, clinical medicine, and food industries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: H2O2 transmits redox signals and plays important roles, including cell proliferation, metabolism, and death [1]. Administration of L-ascorbic acid (AA) conjuncts with the balance of H2O2 [2]. Intracellular AA contributes to decrease high level of H2O2 to a low level [3]. However, the oxidation of ascorbate using metalloprotein favors the production of H2O2 [4]. The in vitro and in vivo activities of H2O2 have been reported with respect to AA [5]. Normally, the concentration of H2O2 is less than 0.1 μM [6] and AA is ∼0.1 mM [7] in organisms. However, the concentration of H2O2 increases to 0.5–50 μM [8] and ∼50 % AA in an injured tissue within 24 h [9]. The equilibria of H2O2 and AA relate to different biochemical processes with different effects. For instance, gram-negative bacteria are considerably sensitive to lysozyme in the presence of H2O2 and AA [10]. AA increases the H2O2 concentration in tumors, facilitating the Fenton reaction to yield more •OH, thereby accelerating the apoptosis of tumor cells [11]. Considerable interest has been generated to track and image H2O2 and AA for clinical applications owing to their ubiquitous presence in living organisms [12,13]. Compared to other methods, the optical method offers the advantages of simplicity, visibility, and sensitivity [14]. It is widely used in pathological diagnosis and tissue imaging [15]. However, the development of optical sensors for H2O2 and AA in organisms is still a challenge owing to their considerable differences in redox potential and reactivity as well as their low concentration in biological systems [16].

Recently, many researchers have expressed interest in developing H2O2 and AA sensors using peroxidase-mimicking nanozymes [17,18]. Such a method mimics a peroxidase catalyzing H2O2 into •OH, coupling the oxidation of aromatic substrates such as 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to result in signal transduction [19,20]. A palygorskite@Co3O4 nanoparticle was used to catalyze the oxidation of TMB to blue oxTMB in the presence of H2O2, and cascade addition of AA reduced oxTMB to TMB with a color variation [21]. A hemoglobin–gold nanoparticle also exhibits peroxidase-like activity under harsh conditions and can be used to detect H2O2, AA, and glucose [22]. Terephthalic acid/CuFe/adenosine triphosphate nanoparticles exhibiting peroxidase-mimicking activity were used to detect H2O2 and AA using o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as a substrate [23]. However, the practical use of these metal-based nanozymes was severely limited in living organisms due to the side effect of the heavy metals [24]. Considerable efforts have been devoted to discovering carbon-based nanozymes with satisfactory biocompatibility and nontoxicity [25].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with a size less than 10 nm, have attracted extensive attention for developing optical sensors owing to their remarkable biocompatibility, bright fluorescence, high solubility, tunable photoluminescence, and high permeability to cells [26,27]. The peroxidase-like functions of GQDs have been previously documented [28]. The –CO groups in GQDs are the primary active sites, and the hydrophobic parts serve as substrate-binding pockets [29] H2O2 detection employing the unique fluorescence of GQDs is not sensitive, which requires a H2O2 concentration of >0.2 mM [30]. Adding a chromogenic agent can considerably enhance the sensitivity. Detection of H2O2 and AA using prepared carbon quantum dots achieved an individual limit of detection (LOD) of 15 and 3.26 μM, respectively, in the presence of TMB [31]. The peroxidase-like activity of GQDs can be enhanced by introducing surface heteroatoms. Such modification alters the electronic structure and electric field distribution of carbon-based materials and reduces the adsorption energy barrier [32]. The luminescent sulfur- and nitrogen-doped GQDs can be used to detect AA at a lower concentration of 1.2 μM [33]. Another research on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots showed an LOD of 1.773 μM for AA in a solution containing H2O2 and TMB [34]. Doping with N in the carbon nonstructural particles enhanced the peroxidase-like activity three folds [35]. However, despite these investigations, studies on nitrogen-doped carbon-based nanozymes are limited. The catalytic mechanism of such nanozymes is still unclear [36]. Additionally, cell imaging is rarely reported because most materials are less permeable and high cytotoxicity as well as because they are not stable when sensing H2O2 and AA in living cells. Hence, we believe more trials are required.

Herein, we synthesize amino-functionalized GQDs (NH2GQDs) as carbon-based nanozymes with intrinsic peroxidase-like properties. In the presence of H2O2, the obtained NH2GQDs exhibit an evident peroxidase activity as testified through electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy. Using the nanozyme property of NH2GQDs, a highly efficient, sensitive, rapid fluorescent, and colorimetric sensing system NH2GQDs/OPD is constructed for the cascade detection of H2O2 and AA (see Scheme 1). OPD is oxidized by H2O2 in the presence of NH2GQDS to produce the oxidative product 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP), which clearly exhibits an emission peak at 560 nm. Simultaneously, the fluorescence intensity of the NH2GQDs at 480 nm is decreased using DAP owing to the inner filter effect (IFE). After adding AA, the fluorescence peak blue shifts from 560 to 510 nm via condensation of DAP with oxidized AA, accompanied by a considerable change in the color of the solution from yellow to green. The optical sensing system can be used to image H2O2 and AA in onion tissues and human cervical cancer (Hela) cells. Furthermore, constructing a logic gate clearly identifies the copresence of H2O2 and AA in a solution or biosystem. We believe that the results provide a valuable strategy for the practical applications of carbon-based nanozymes in the imaging of biochemical events.

All chemicals were of analytical grade and were directly used without further purification. Graphite powder was acquired from Qingdao Haida Graphite Co., Ltd. All chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (China). All aqueous solutions used in the experiments were freshly prepared using Millipore ultrapure water.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were collected using an Agilent Cary 100 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an ESCALB MK-II spectrometer. Raman spectra were recorded on a Renishaw 2000 equipped with an Ar-ion laser at an excitation line of 514.5 nm and an air-cooling, charge-coupled device as the detector. Fluorescence measurements were performed on a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a plotter unit and a 1-cm quartz cell. Fluorescence spectra were recorded with bandwidths of 5 nm for both excitation and emission wavelengths. Liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) was conducted on an Agilent 6545 LC/Q-TOF. The molecular mass spectrum was recorded using the Waters XEVO G2-XS Q-TOF spectrometer equipped with a Spray ESI source.

NH2GQDs were synthesized by following the synthesis procedure from a previous study [37], which includes the acid-oxidation procedure followed by filtration and dialysis. In a typical preparation experiment, 7.5 mL of graphene oxide (GO) (2 mg/mL) and 5 mL of ammonia solution (28 wt%) were mixed for a 0.5 h ultrasonic treatment. Thereafter, hydrothermal treatment was conducted in an autoclave at 180 ℃ for 12 h. The solution was filtered using a 0.2 µm membrane upon cooling to room temperature and was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 0.5 h to remove the macroparticles. Finally, the yellow supernatant was heated at 100 ℃ for 1 h to evaporate the unreacted ammonia. When the temperature naturally decreased to 25 ℃, the obtained solution was dialyzed for 72 h using a dialysis bag with 500-Da cut-off molecular weight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the NH2GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase properties of the NH2GQDs were measured in the presence of TMB and H2O2. Briefly, the NH2GQDs (200 μg/mL, 200 μL) and TMB (5 mM, 100 μL) were added to 2.7 mL of acetic acid buffer (pH = 4). Thereafter, 0.1 mL of 100 mM H2O2 was added to the abovementioned mixture solution, followed by incubation for 0.5 h at room temperature. UV–vis absorption spectra were collected using an Agilent Cary 100 spectrometer.

First, OPD and the NH2GQDs were dissolved and dispersed in a 10 mM phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 6.6). Then, 500 μL of 0.01-M OPD, 750 μL of NH2GQDs, and 10 μL of variable concentrations of H2O2 were effectively mixed. The resulting mixed solution was incubated at 37 °C in the dark for 0.5 h. The fluorescence spectra were measured under 400-nm excitation for the quantitative analysis of H2O2. It has been well-known that pure solutions of H2O2 is stable at low pH [38]. The commercial 30 % H2O2 solution is with a pH of 3.5. It was diluted directly and the accurate concentration was determined by titration using a standard KMnO4 solution. The freshly prepared H2O2 solutions were used quickly.

For the detection of AA, 300 μL of different concentrations of AA were added into a mixture solution of NH2GQDs (750 μL, 200 μg/mL), OPD (500 μL, 0.01-M OPD), and H2O2 (100 mM, 40 μL). The resulting solutions were incubated at 37 °C in the dark for 0.5 h before conducting spectra measurement. The fluorescence spectra were obtained at 400-nm excitation. Quantum yield was determined based on a previous procedure using Rhodamine 6 G as reference (literature quantum yield 95 % at 488 nm) [39]. Although AA solution is stable in 3 day [40], dilute AA is vulnerable and the freshly prepared solution must be assayed quickly.

The cell viability was evaluated using Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8 assay) after culturing the NH2GQDs with Hela cells. In the experiments, different concentrations of NH2GQDs and different cell incubation times were adopted, as described in a previous study [41].

First, the Hela cells were cultured at 37 °C in an incubator for 24 h using 24-well plates. The cells were incubated with the NH2GQDs for 2 h and washed thrice with PBS. Imaging of the cells was recorded at 450–510 nm (blue channel).

Then, the Hela cells were incubated with the NH2GQDs for 2 h and washed thrice with PBS, followed by successively adding OPD/H2O2 into the culture solution for incubation for another 0.5 h. Imaging of the cells was recorded at 450–510 nm (blue channel) and 550–610 nm (red channel).

Finally, the Hela cells were incubated with the NH2GQDs for 2 h and washed thrice with PBS, followed by successively adding OPD/H2O2/AA into the culture solution for incubation for another 0.5 h. Imaging of the cells was recorded at 510–550 nm (green channel) and 550–610 nm (red channel).

Onion tissues were cut into small pieces (0.5 cm × 0.5 cm) and placed in a 24-well plate. The incubation procedure of the onion tissues was similar to that of the Hela cells. The onion tissues were treated with the NH2GQDs for 0.5 h, followed by successively adding OPD/H2O2 or OPD/H2O2/AA to the culture solution for another 0.5 h. The onion tissues were then washed thrice with PBS before imaging. Images were collected at 450–510 nm (blue channel), 510–550 nm (green channel), and 550–610 nm (red channel).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical application of the NH2GQD/OPD/H2O2 optical sensing system for AA detection in tap water, drink, and human serum was evaluated through a fluorescence assay. The juice was purchased from a local supermarket (Wenzhou, China) and filtered with a 0.25 µm filter membrane before use. The human serum was obtained from the Second Affiliated Hospital of Wenzhou Medical University. The serum was ultrafiltered (0.25 µm pore size filter membrane) and diluted 100-fold. The samples were spiked with different concentrations of standard AA after incubation of the abovementioned mixed solution for 0.5 h (2 mL pH = 4 acetate buffer, 750 μL of 200 μg/mL NH2GQDs, 250 μL of 5 mM OPD, and 40 μL of 100 mM H2O2). The recovery results were achieved by measurements conducted in triplicate. Also, the content of AA in juice was determined using LC-MS.

The NH2GQDs were prepared using a hydrothermal method with GO and NH3·H2O as the reactants. The NH2GQDs possessed a dotted structure with a diameter of ∼10 nm and were uniformly dispersed in the aqueous solution, as shown in Fig. 1a. The Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of the NH2GQDs exhibited a wide absorption band at 3130 cm−1, which was attributed to N–H stretching vibrations. The peaks at 1400 and 1130 cm−1 were ascribed to C–N and C–O bending vibrations, respectively (see Fig. 1b). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the NH2GQDs displayed a broad peak centered at ∼23°, which was ascribed to highly disordered carbon atoms (see Fig. 1c). The Raman spectrum of the NH2GQDs displayed typical D and G bands at 1330 and 1611 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 1d). The D band reflected the defect or edge of the carbon atom lattice. Meanwhile, the G band represented an ordered band, indicating the sp2 hybridization of carbon atoms in the plane. The ratio of the intensities of the two bands (ID/IG) was 1.16, indicating that the NH2GQDs contained sp2 carbon hybridization and partially defective structure in the carbon atom lattice [42].

The chemical valence states and composition of the NH2GQDs were further confirmed via XPS characterization. The peaks at 285, 402, and 532 eV represented C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively, as shown in Fig. S1. The C1s spectrum displayed four peaks at 284.6, 284.6, 286.0, and 288.5 eV, assigned to the functional groups C–C/CC, C–N, C-O, and CO, respectively. In the high resolution of the N1s spectrum, peaks were displayed at 401.1, 401.5, and 402.0 eV, revealing the presence of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and -NH2, respectively. In the high resolution of the O1s spectrum, peaks displayed at 531.3, 531.9, and 533.3 eV were assigned to the functional groups C–O–C, CO, and C–OH, respectively (see Fig. 1h) [43]. The C/N molar ratio was calculated to be 12.27:1 (see Table S1). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the NH2GQDs (see Fig. S2a) exhibited two notable absorption bands at 230 and 270 nm, which were attributed to the π–π*transition of CC and the n–π*transition of CO/CN [44]. These results indicated the formation of the NH2GQDs. The fluorescence of NH2GQDs were continuously measured once a month for 6 months. It can be found that the fluorescence intensity of NH2GQDs slightly decreased in the second month and subsequently remained stable (Fig. S2b). There was no obvious precipitation at the bottom of the bottle after placing NH2GQDs solution for six months.

The peroxidase-like property of the NH2GQDs was investigated using TMB as the typical colorimetric substrate. Fig. S3 shows the chemical structure changes of TMB in the catalytic process. The UV–vis absorption spectra and solution color changes of different systems (NH2GQDs, TMB, NH2GQDs/H2O2, TMB/H2O2, TMB/NH2GQDs, and TMB/NH2GQDs/H2O2) were measured and imaged (see Fig. 2). The NH2GQDs could catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into the radical •OH as identified via electron paramagnetic resonance characterization using 2,3-dimethylpyriodine-N-oxide (DMPO) as the probe. The typical four lines of the radical DMPO• were found in the NH2GQD/H2O2 system, indicating the formation of •OH radicals in the process (see Fig. 2a) [45]. For the UV–vis examination (Fig. 2b), no obvious absorption peak at 652 nm was observed in the NH2GQDs, TMB, NH2GQDs/H2O2, TMB/H2O2, and TMB/NH2GQD systems. Meanwhile, the TMB/NH2GQD/H2O2 system exhibited evident oxTMB absorption at 652 nm [46] with the appearance of strong blue color (Fig. 2c), while the other systems exhibited no obvious color change or partial color changes, which confirmed that the NH2GQDs exhibited strong peroxidase-like characteristics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: H2O2 and AA sensing mechanism using OPD as the chromogenic agent
ce:section.ce:para: Using OPD as the chromogenic agent, the NH2GQD/OPD system can be used to detect H2O2 and AA in sequence using colorimetric and fluorescence methods. The dual determination methods make the detection of H2O2 and AA considerably flexible, visible, and accurate. The NH2GQD/OPD system exhibited evident absorption peaks at 230 and 265 nm, as shown in Fig. S4. In the presence of H2O2, the peak at 265 nm disappeared and two new ones appeared at 285 and 420 nm. The latter two peaks had typical characteristics of DAP as previously reported [47]. Upon further addition of AA to the NH2GQD/OPD/H2O2 solution, the UV–vis spectrum exhibited another peak at 345 nm. In such a case, AA was oxidized to dehydroascorbic acid, which was then condensed with OPD or DAP, forming complicated compounds [48]. It was observed that the color of the incubated solution exhibited the instant change in color, and reached an equilibrium within 0.5 h. At 400 nm excitation, the NH2GQD system exhibited an emission peak at 480 nm, while the NH2GQD/OPD/H2O2 system displayed a fluorescent peak at 560 nm (see Fig. S4b). It is well-known that the emission peak at 560 nm is assigned to the formation of the oxidative product DAP [49]. The OPD was oxidized to the strongly fluorescent DAP by the radical •OH in the presence of NH2GQDs/H2O2. DAP is a typical aromatic ring molecule with electron-deficient properties, which can easily interact with the electron-rich NH2GQDs via hydrogen bonding and π–π interaction, forming NH2GQDs/DAP. Upon excitation at 400 nm, the DAP strongly adsorbed light, which reduced the emission of the NH2GQDs, forming an IFE course [50]. Upon adding AA into the resulting NH2GQD/OPD/H2O2 solution, the fluorescent peak shifted to 510 nm from 560 nm and the intensity increased. The fluorescent peak at 510 nm differed from the emission peak of 3-(dihydroxyethyl)furo[3,4-b]quinoxaline-1-one (OPD with dehydroascorbic acid), which appeared at ∼435 nm [51]. The quadrupole time-of-flight mass spectrometry of the NH2GQD/OPD/H2O2/AA solution presented a mass-to-charge ratio (m/z) of 367.1061, which is consistent with that of the DAP and dehydroascorbic acid condensation species (see Fig. S5). Additionally, 3-(dihydroxyethyl)furo[3,4-b]quinoxaline-1-one was observed at an m/z of 265.1088 with low relative abundance. Despite the AA has different isomers, only their oxidation product, dehydroascorbic acid readily condensed with DAP or OPD. Without H2O2, the NH2GQD/OPD/AA solution exhibited an emission peak at 490 nm similar to the NH2GQDs. The fluorescence lifetimes of the NH2GQD/OPD, NH2GQD/OPD/H2O2, and NH2GQD/OPD/H2O2/AA systems are 4.21, 4.71, and 4.86, respectively (Fig. S6), agreeing with the behavior of individual fluorescence. Although the oxidation of OPD could be triggered by other ROS such as ClO– or ONOO–, the fluorescent species were different due to complexation of DAP with additional fluorophore (acting as catalyst). Therefore, the OPD/catalyst system constructed a sensing system targeted to a certain ROS and produced a specific optical signal. Also, the quantum yield of different sensing system differed. It has been reported that ClO– quenched the fluorescence of GQDs/OPD at 520 nm with a quantum yield of 6.2 % [52]. A carbon dots-triphenylphosphonium/OPD system sensed ONOO– at 560 nm [53]. And the boric acid-protected L-dopa gold nanoclusters/OPD platform detected •OH, ClO–, and ONOO– at 480 nm with a quantum yield of 2.8 % [54]. Their behaviors are different from NH2GQD/OPD due to difference in nanocatalyst. The quantum yields of NH2GQD/OPD/H2O2 and NH2GQD/OPD/H2O2/AA are 11.22 % and 12.97 %, respectively. These facts suggest that the NH2GQD/OPD system can be used for cascade detection of H2O2 and AA.

The impact of different pH values on the NH2GQDs/OPD sensing system was shown in Fig. S2c. The fluorescence intensity continuously increases with the increase of pH value within the pH 4.0–7.0. The fluorescence intensity of NH2GQDs/OPD evidently diminished from pH 7.0 to pH 9.0. Furthermore, H2O2 is relatively stable in weak acidic solution as mentioned above. Weak acidic solution was suitable for fluorescence detection, and the pH between 6 and 7 was better.

The fluorescence intensity of NH2GQDs/OPD at 560 nm gradually increased with increasing H2O2 concentration, which is attributed to the DAP concentration increasing on account of successively increasing the concentration of the radical •OH (see Fig. 3a). The color of the NH2GQD/OPD solution gradually changed from colorless to yellow upon adding different concentrations of H2O2. Moreover, the darkening of the solution was observed to be dependent on H2O2 concentration. Meanwhile, the intensity of the peak at 480 nm decreased owing to the IFE mechanism, as previously mentioned. The linear calibration curve was shown within 4.75–58.82 μM with the regression equation: (F – F0)/ F0 = 0.0234 × C H2O2 + 0.45 (R2 = 0.9931) (see Fig. 3b). The LOD was calculated to be 0.207 μM (3σ/K). The UV–vis absorption peak of NH2GQDs/OPD at 420 nm evidently increased with increasing H2O2 concentration from 0 to 7.94 μM, accompanied by a weak blue shift, indicating that redox and electron transition had occurred (Fig. 3e). The linear regression equation was y = 0.0132 × C H2O2 + 0.175 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9842. The LOD achieved was 0.176 μM as calculated via UV–vis spectroscopy.

Upon continuous addition of AA, the titration curve of the NH2GQD/OPD/H2O2 sensing system for AA detection was obtained. The peak at 560 nm shifted to 510 nm, and the intensity enhanced with increasing AA concentration, as shown in Fig. 3c. The color of the NH2GQD/OPD/H2O2 solution gradually changed from yellow to green. A satisfactorily linear relation was obtained between relative fluorescence intensity and AA concentration within the range of 0.50–4.75 μM. The regression equation was (F – F0) / F0 = 0.475 × CAA –0.216 (R2 = 0.9908) (see Fig. 3d). Additionally, the LOD was 0.062 μM based on 3σ/K. The UV–vis absorption spectra of NH2GQDs/OPD/H2O2 displayed an obvious red shift with increasing AA concentration (Fig. 3f), which can be attributed to molecular condensations. The linear regression equation was y = 0.022 × CAA + 0.088 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9836 within the range of 0–4.59 μM. The LOD was calculated to be 0.057 μM.

Reportedly, dopamine also responded to Fe3+-catalyzed OPD in a H2O2 solution [55]. Glucose can be detected using glucose oxidase/copper-doped carbon-based nanozyme/Mg–N-doped carbon quantum dots with DAP [56]. However, such reduction biomolecules do not affect the AA responding to NH2GQDs/OPD in the presence of H2O2. Most of the reduction substances experienced a process of quinoxaline formation with fluorescence at 435 nm. For comparison, other common biomolecules and ions were tested under identical conditions, including dopamine, glucose, cysteine, fructose, glucosamine, glucose, galactose, glucuronic acid, glutathione, Al3+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, and Fe3+, which did not exhibit a turn-on fluorescence response (see Fig. S7). Noteworthily, the fluorescence response of NH2GQDs/OPD/H2O2 was observed after adding AA in the presence of five times more concentrated biomolecules and cations. The primary reason may be the formation of the condensation compound of DAP with dehydroascorbic acid rather than the quinoxaline path as previously described. These results evidently showed the high selectivity of NH2GQDs/OPD/H2O2 toward AA, even in the presence of interferences. Table S2 compares the detection performance of the NH2GQD/OPD/H2O2 sensing system for AA with previously reported sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of H2O2 and AA in Hela cells
ce:section.ce:para: The CCK8 assay was performed on Hela cells to investigate the biocompatibility and potential toxicity of the NH2GQDs. The Hela cells were incubated with the NH2GQDs at different concentrations and for different incubation periods. The results showed that for incubations of 24 and 72 h, the cell viability was more than 95 % and 55 % at a high concentration (200 μg/mL), respectively (Fig. S8). The biocompatibility of the NH2GQDs was further evaluated via the live/dead cell double-staining method using calcein-AM and propidium iodide (PI) as the stains, respectively (Fig. S9–S11). The Hela cells were cultured with the NH2GQDs under different concentrations (50, 100, 150, and 200 μg/mL) for 24, 48, and 72 h. After incubation with the NH2GQDs for 24 h using PI staining, the cell viability was more than 90 % at a concentration of 200 μg/mL. At the same concentration, the cell viability exceeded 70 % and 55 % after incubation for 48 and 72 h, respectively. These results indicate satisfactory biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity of the NH2GQDs.

The feasibility of the NH2GQDs/OPD as optical sensors for monitoring H2O2 and AA in biological systems was further investigated. The practical application capability of the sensing system was explored using Hela cells through dual-channel confocal fluorescence experiments. The corresponding bright-field images and overlayed images are shown in Fig. 4a, e, and i and Fig. 4d, h, and l, respectively. The cells incubated in normal culture fluid (without NH2GQDs, OPD, H2O2, and AA) gave faint images (Fig. S12) in blue and red fields. The cells incubated with the NH2GQDs exhibited a bright blue fluorescence signal (Fig. 4b), while no visible red fluorescence signal was observed (see Fig. 4c). After successively adding OPD and H2O2 to the culture medium, the blue fluorescence signal of the cells gradually weakened and a considerable red fluorescence appeared (Fig. 4f and g). Moreover, the red fluorescence signal gradually enhanced with the increasing concentration of H2O2 (Fig. 4j and k), suggesting that the NH2GQDs/OPD can be employed as a useful visible sensor to track intracellular H2O2.

The AA images in living Hela cells were also identifiable. The corresponding bright-field images and overlayed images are shown in Fig. 5a, e, and i and Fig. 5d, h, and l, respectively. With increasing AA concentration, the green fluorescence intensity of the cells was continuously enhanced (Fig. 5c, g, and 5k). However, the red fluorescence intensity gradually decreased until almost invisible (Fig. 5b, f, and 5j). This phenomenon suggests that the NH2GQD/OPD/H2O2 can be used as a fluorescent sensor to monitor AA in living cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of H2O2 and AA in onion tissues
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, the confocal microscopic imaging of onion tissues was performed after incubating them with NH2GQDs at 37 ℃ for 0.5 h. The bright-field images and overlayed images are shown in Fig. S13a, S13e, S13i, S14a, S14e, S14i, S13d, S13h, S13l, S14d, S14h, and S14i. Clearly, the onion tissues exhibited bright fluorescence in the blue channel (450–510 nm), while no visible fluorescence was noted in the red channel (Fig. S13b and S13c). After successively adding OPD and H2O2 to the medium and incubating for another 0.5 h, the intensity of blue fluorescence signal evidently decreased and fluorescence was observed in the red channel (550–610 nm) (Fig. S13f and S13j). The fluorescence intensity in the red channel increased with the increasing concentration of H2O2 (Fig. S13g and S13k).

Before exploring AA detection in onion tissues, we first incubated them with NH2GQDs/OPD/H2O2 for 0.5 h at 37 ℃. The confocal microscopy results showed that a notable red fluorescence was observed in the red channel and no fluorescence was observed in the green channel (Fig. S14b and S14c). Thereafter, we added AA to the abovementioned onion tissues and incubated them for another 0.5 h, following which the intensity of red fluorescence signal evidently decreased (Fig. S14f and S14j). The onion tissues displayed an evident fluorescence in the green channel (510–550 nm), as shown in Fig. S14g. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity was obviously enhanced after treatment with various AA concentrations. These results indicate that NH2GQD/OPD/H2O2, as an optical sensor, was tissue penetrable and can be used to detect AA content in biological systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in tap water, human serum, and drink
ce:section.ce:para: Spike-recovery experiments were conducted by adding known AA concentrations to real samples (tap water, human serum, and drink) to assess the practical application and accuracy of NH2GQDs/OPD/H2O2 for AA detection. The data showed that all recoveries ranged between 94.29 % and 108 %, and the relative standard deviation was less than 4.48 % (Table S3). Therefore, the assay could be used to rapidly detect AA concentration in tap water, serum, and drink with high credibility. These results suggest that NH2GQDs/OPD/H2O2 can be used as a reliable method for detecting AA in real samples. It has been reported that the OPD was stable in serum at least 1 h [57]. As mentioned above, it has sufficient time to perform in serum solutions.

Based on the sensing behavior of the NH2GQD/OPD/sensing system toward H2O2 and AA, the logic gate “AND” was successfully constructed using OPD, H2O2, and AA as the inputs (see Fig. S15a). The presence of OPD (input 1), H2O2 (input 2), and AA (input 3) was set as “1” and their absence as “0,” as shown in Fig. S15b. Meanwhile, three outputs were color (output 1), normalized fluorescence intensity (normalized FL) (output 2), and peak shift (output 3). The normalized FL value 1.1 was defined as the threshold value (see Fig. S15d). The normalized FL value more than and less than the threshold value was assumed to be “1” and “0,” respectively. For the outputs, “1” was defined as the “on” state, which corresponded to the nonblue solution under a UV lamp, above the threshold and peak shift (relative to 480 nm). Meanwhile, “0” was defined as the “off” state, which corresponded to the blue solution under a UV lamp, below the threshold value and negligible peak shift.

The possible outputs obtained by combining various inputs are shown in Fig. S15b. In the absence of any inputs (0, 0, 0), the output was represented by (0, 0, 0). When AA input was alone or presented with H2O2, no outputs were observed, which was represented by (0, 0, 0). In the presence of OPD alone or with AA, no outputs were observed, which produced the output state (0, 0, 0). In the presence of H2O2 alone, no evident effect on the fluorescence intensity was observed, with the generated output state being (0, 0, 0). However, in the presence of OPD and H2O2, the color of the solution changed from blue to yellow, with the output state produced being (1, 0, 0). Meanwhile, in the presence of the three inputs (1, 1, 1), the corresponding output state was denoted by (1, 1, 1). These results confirm that the behavior of NH2GQDs/OPD/H2O2 in detecting H2O2 and AA can be used to construct higher logic systems with triple inputs and outputs.

A fluorescent and colorimetric sensing system, NH2GQD/OPD system, was successfully constructed for cascade detection of H2O2 and AA. The sensing system has the advantages of simple synthesis, easy handle, and good biocompatibility. NH2GQDs exhibited peroxidase-like catalytic properties and could rapidly catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 to produce radical •OH. The NH2GQD/OPD system was employed for cascade detection of H2O2 and AA with high sensitivity and high selectivity. Total time of detection record in one cycle is 1 h. The fluorescence LOD was calculated as 0.207 and 0.062 μM for H2O2 and AA, respectively. Importantly, the sensing system was successfully applied to monitor AA in living cells and onion tissues via fluorescence imaging. Noteworthily, the proposed fluorescent assay was used to detect AA in tap water, human serum, and juice samples with satisfactory recoveries. Furthermore, the logic gate “AND” with three inputs and three outputs was constructed. These results indicate that NH2GQDs/OPD, as a dual optical sensor, has impressive prospects for cascade detection of H2O2 and AA in complex biological samples, emphasizing its potential application in medical diagnosis and biosensing.

Wencan Wu: Conceptualization, formal analysis, supervision, project administration, funding acquisition. Yixuan Xu: Titration experiments. C. Zhou and R. Cheng contributed equally. ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chaoqun Zhou studied material science at the South China University of Technology and received her master’s degree. In 2020, she became a PhD candidate in the Laboratory for Nanotechnology Applications at Wenzhou Medical University. Her current research topics include construction and logic gate operation of fluorescence detection system for biological small molecule recognition using graphene quantum dots.

Prof. Dr. Rumei Cheng studied chemistry at Tongji University and received her PhD. She has been working at the School of Optometry of Wenzhou Medical University since 2010. She focuses on design and fabrication of nanostructural optical sensors for disease diagnostics. From 2019–2020, she was involved in lab-on-a-chip-research and the development of molecular diagnostic techniques at University of New South Wales, Sydney. In 2020, she became a group leader of Carbon-based Optical Nanodevices for Sensors at the School of Optometry of Wenzhou Medical University.

Liu Bin studied computer science and technology in Jiangxi Agricultural University. In his bachelor's thesis, he studied the development of online fruit mall system. He is now a graduate student in the School of Ophthalmology of Wenzhou Medical University.

Yuying Fang studied pharmaceutical engineering at Anhui University of Science and Technology. In 2021, she started her master at the Laboratory for Nanotechnology Applications at Wenzhou Medical University.

Prof. Dr. Kaihui Nan received a doctor's degree in materials science and engineering from South China University of Technology in 2005. He worked in the post-doctoral station of clinical medicine of Southern Medical University and entered the School of Optometry of Wenzhou Medical University in October 2007. Then he visited to the Shiley Eye Center at the University of California, San Diego, USA from 2012 to 2014. Now, he is director of Wenzhou Biomaterials Engineering Technology Research Center and editorial member of the Chinese Journal of Biomedical Engineering. He was awarded Young and middle-aged discipline leader of Zhejiang Province, China. He focuses in the eye tissue engineering, intraocular drug delivery, and the development of biomaterials by surface modification.

Prof. Dr. Wencan Wu is currently dean of the School of Optometry of Wenzhou Medical University. He is chief expert of the "National Key Research and Development Program" and Fellow of the American Bascom Palmer Eye Institute. He also serves as the deputy editor of China Endoscopy Journal. In 2004, he obtained his doctor’s degree from Xiangya School of Medicine at Central South University, and entered Wenzhou Medical University. He was selected as "Outstanding Talents in Wenzhou City", "High level Innovative Talents in Health in Zhejiang Province", and "151 New Century Talents in Zhejiang Province". He became the founder of minimally invasive ophthalmic endoscopic surgery in China.
Yixuan Xu is an undergraduate student in College of Biological Science at UC Davis. His interest is bioimaging.
